title: The Sorrows Of A Simple Bard
content:
آ آ آ آ When I tell a tale of virtue and of injured innocence,
آ آ آ آ Then my publishers and lawyers are the densest of the dense:
آ آ آ آ With the blank face of an image and the nod of keep-it-dark
آ آ آ آ And a wink of mighty meaning at their confidential clerk.

آ آ آ آ (When, Oh! tell me when shall poets cease to be misunderstood?
آ آ آ آ When, Oh! When? shall people reckon rhymers can be any good?
آ آ آ آ Do their work and pay their debts and drink their pint of beer, and then,
آ آ آ آ Look in womanï؟½s eyes and leave them, just like ordinary men?)

آ آ آ آ ï؟½Is there literary friendship ï؟½twix the sexes? donï؟½t you think?ï؟½
آ آ آ آ And they wink their idiotic and exasperating wink.
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Canï؟½t we kiss a clever woman without wanting any more?ï؟½
آ آ آ آ And their clock-work nod is only more decided than before.

آ آ آ آ But if I should hint that thereï؟½s a little woman somewhere, say,
آ آ آ آ Then the public and the law are interested straight away,
آ آ آ آ The impassive confidential gets a bright and cheerful glance,
آ آ آ آ Things are straightway on a footing that may lead to an advance.

آ آ آ آ Both are married and respected and they both are rising higher:
آ آ آ آ Oneï؟½s church warden, oneï؟½s a deacon in a fashionable choir.
آ آ آ آ And the clerks have both unblemished private characters to show,
آ آ آ آ What do they know about woman? Thatï؟½s what I should like to know.

آ آ آ آ (Flash of dark eyes in the moonlight, in the scrub or far afield,
آ آ آ آ Blouse-sleeves back from white arms clinging, clinging while she will not yield,
آ آ آ آ Or the fair head on your shoulder and the grey eyes moist and mild,
آ آ آ آ Weary of the strife with passion, yielding like a tired child.)

آ آ آ آ Thereï؟½s my aunt; the dear old lady hints about ï؟½experienceï؟½
آ آ آ آ When I go to her for comfort with my injured innocence.
آ آ آ آ She screws up a wise expression, while she listens, for my pains,
آ آ آ آ Isnï؟½t it an awful pity women havenï؟½t any brains?

آ آ آ آ Now Iï؟½m serious and angry, for it isnï؟½t any joke,
آ آ آ آ Poets have been damned for ages by such evil-minded folk.
آ آ آ آ Must we all be public blackguards? Canï؟½t a rhymer be a man,
آ آ آ آ Spite of Byronï؟½s silly mistress, Burnsï؟½s gawky Mary Ann?

آ آ آ آ As tame bards they will not have us, and I donï؟½t know what they want,
آ آ آ آ Thereï؟½s my publisher and lawyer, my admirers and my aunt.
آ آ آ آ Do they want a rake and a spendthrift? Look out! Tradesman trusting me!
آ آ آ آ Look out! Husbands! Fathers! Brothers! Iï؟½ll be wicked as can be!
آ آ آ آ There now.
meaning: About: Henry Lawson (17 June 1867 - 2 September 1922) was an Australian writer and poet. Along with his contemporary Banjo Paterson, Lawson is among the best-known Australian poets and fiction writers of the colonial period.
